# Brief: Maria (PERSONA-001)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-07

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence arrived between the April 2 school board meeting and tonight's city council meeting. The gap is five days. Nothing has been withheld; the district's next formal step was always this council meeting, which marks the deadline the school board has been working toward.

What matters as background for tonight: as of April 2, the FY27 school budget had still not been adopted by the school board. The board was waiting for a firmer state funding number before voting. Tonight is the April 7 city council deadline — the date the board has treated as the outer limit for transmitting a budget. Maria arrives not knowing whether the school board convened between April 2 and tonight, whether it passed a budget, and at what dollar figure.

---

## Open Questions

Maria carries these into the meeting:

- **Did the school board actually adopt a budget before this deadline?** If not, what happens tonight — does the council proceed anyway, delay, or send something back?
- **Which positions, if any, are being restored with the $300,000 in new state aid?** Will any of those restorations go to behavioral support or MTSS staff, or only to classroom teachers?
- **When do we find out where our kids are going?** Attendance boundaries under Option A still haven't been drawn. If I have a kindergartner and a third-grader, I still don't know if they'll be in the same building.
- **Is anyone planning for the behavioral crisis that hits in September?** The district is eliminating the only general education behavioral strategist — the person with nearly 60 students and 40+ formal behavior plans — at exactly the moment Kayler kids arrive at unfamiliar schools with bigger classes and no aides.
- **What number is the city actually working from — $7.2M or $8.4M?** The gap grew on the record, and I still don't have a clean answer on where the higher figure comes from.
- **Will the one state waiver day be enough?** The district traded four early release planning days for a single waiver day. That's one day to prepare staff for reconfiguring every elementary school in the district by September.
- **Is the district tracking ICE-related absences separately?** Or is that displacement just getting absorbed into the "enrollment decline" numbers without anyone naming it?
- **What is the actual net benefit to schools from the 170 Ocean TIF deal?** The city manager referenced state subsidy formula changes, but I've never seen the math spelled out in a form that makes sense to me.
- **Is anyone building a housing-density enrollment model?** The state is mandating more density. If those families have kids, the staffing cuts being finalized now may not fit the school population we'll actually have in two or three years.

---

## Agenda Implications

### G.2 — Budget Presentation and Public Hearing (the central item)

This is why Maria is here tonight. The city manager is presenting the full city budget, and this is the formal public hearing on it. The timeline embedded in this agenda item is the clearest statement Maria has seen of how the remaining decisions unfold:

- **April 14**: Budget Workshop #1 — School is listed *first* among the departments reviewed. This is Maria's next real opportunity to get specific answers about elementary programs, position restorations, and the reconfiguration transition plan. Tonight is not that meeting.
- **May 5**: Public hearing and council vote to send the school budget to voters.
- **June 9**: School budget referendum.

**What to listen for tonight:** The presentation will establish the city manager's framing of the school budget — the number being carried forward, whether the state aid figure has been firmed up, and whether any language about position restorations appears in the presentation. If the school board did not pass a budget before tonight, the city manager will need to address that procedurally. Watch for any acknowledgment of a gap, a contingency, or a motion to extend the deadline.

**What to prepare:** Maria should prepare a public comment for the hearing portion. This is the moment to put specific concerns on the record — specifically the behavioral strategist elimination and MTSS cuts — before the April 14 school workshop. She should name those positions by function, not by line item, so the record is clear.

**Question to ask during public comment:** "When the school budget comes to the April 14 workshop, will there be a line-by-line accounting of which positions are proposed for restoration using the new state aid, and will that include behavioral and MTSS support staff — not only classroom teachers?"

---

### H.1 — Postponed Order (originally from March 3 and March 19)

This item has been postponed twice and is now returning with an amended order and an entirely new alternative order that supersedes the original March recommendation. The agenda does not identify the subject of this order by name, so Maria cannot know its content from the agenda text alone.

**Why this matters:** The two postponements, the red-lined amendments, and the replacement alternative order suggest this is a substantive and contested policy matter that the council has struggled to resolve. Given that both March 3 and March 19 meetings included significant school- and housing-related business, this order may intersect with concerns Maria is tracking.

**What to do:** Arrive early enough to read the position paper at the meeting, or look up ORDER #135-24/25 in the city's online records before 6 PM. If this order touches on housing stability, immigrant family support, or any funding mechanism that affects school enrollment, Maria should be ready to track the vote. If it is unrelated to her concerns, she can deprioritize it.

---

### D — Board and Committee Vacancies

The agenda lists six vacancies or anticipated vacancies across unnamed boards and committees, including resignations from Lisa Maxfield (January), Michael Duvernay (January), and Alan Mills (March), plus three anticipated vacancies from terms expiring May 4.

**Why this matters to Maria:** Without knowing which boards these vacancies are on, she cannot assess their direct relevance. However, she should note whether any of these seats are on committees with budget or schools oversight. The council may move quickly to fill or defer them tonight.

**What to listen for:** If any vacancy is identified as being on a finance, education, or budget committee, and the council is considering an appointment, Maria should register whether the candidate has any stated position on school funding.

---

### E — Consent Calendar (Orders and Grants)

The consent calendar includes several items: standing orders, an amended board policy, a highway safety speed grant, a Maine Office of Community Affairs grant, a pool deck flooring bid (SPCC), and an SMCC shoreway agreement.

**Why this matters:** Consent items are typically passed as a block without discussion. The Maine Office of Community Affairs grant and the SPCC pool deck item are the only ones that could touch public facility or community services budgets. None appear directly school-related on their face.

**What to watch:** If any council member pulls an item off consent for separate discussion, listen for whether the rationale involves budget tradeoffs that implicate the school side.

---

### G.3 and G.4 — Marijuana Ordinances

Two ordinance amendments related to marijuana. Not relevant to Maria's elementary school concerns.

---

### H.2–H.4 — E-Bike Ordinances

Three ordinances regulating e-bikes in parks, traffic, and recreational spaces. Not relevant to Maria's concerns tonight.

---

### H.8 — PPLC License Renewal Comment Letter

The council is voting to submit a comment letter to Maine DEP on a pipeline license renewal covering environmental and financial assurance matters. Not relevant to Maria's concerns tonight.

## Watch For

- **Whether the city manager's budget presentation names a specific school budget figure** — and whether that figure reflects a board-adopted budget or a projected placeholder. If the school board never adopted a budget before tonight's deadline, the presentation may reference a figure without board authorization. That's a procedural signal worth noting.

- **Whether the $300,000 in new state aid appears in the presentation, and whether any position restoration is mentioned alongside it.** If the city manager mentions state aid but says nothing about what it restores, that's a gap Maria should surface in public comment or note as a question for the April 14 workshop.

- **The specific departmental order in the April 14 workshop.** The agenda lists School *first* among the departments in Workshop #1. That means the school portion will be heard when the council is freshest and least fatigued. Maria should plan to attend the full April 14 workshop and prioritize arriving on time — that is a better venue for substantive line-item questioning than tonight's public hearing.

- **Whether any council member asks the city manager about the state funding confirmation that was holding up the school board vote.** If a councilor raises this, listen for the answer. A firm state number being on the record tonight would clarify whether the board can finally adopt the budget.

- **DeAngelis's presence or absence at tonight's meeting.** She is not a council member, but if school board representatives attend, listen for whether any of them speak to the budget status, the state aid, or position restorations during public comment. This would give Maria information about where the board stands that she doesn't otherwise have.

- **The tone of council members when the school budget is referenced.** At the March 19 meeting, school budget language was used as rhetorical backdrop while the council advanced the capital bond. Tonight is the formal budget presentation. Watch for whether councilors engage the school portion with specificity — asking about the behavioral strategist, MTSS, or class size impacts — or again treat it as a line item in the aggregate tax burden calculation.

- **Any mention of the April 14 school workshop format.** If the city manager indicates whether families and staff may testify at the April 14 workshop, or whether it will be staff-and-council-only, Maria needs to know that now to plan whether to show up.

- **H.1 content when it is discussed.** If this postponed order turns out to be the housing or immigrant family assistance order Maria has been tracking, watch how the council characterizes the connection between housing stability and school enrollment. Councilor Scott made this argument explicitly in March. Whether others follow or resist it tonight tells Maria which councilors have internalized that framing — and which she needs to continue pressing.

- **Public comment turnout from other parents.** Maria is an information multiplier in parent networks. If other parents show up tonight with similar concerns, that's a data point about whether the organizing she has been doing has reached people, and whether the April 14 workshop is likely to draw a crowd worth coordinating.
